summary: Jeff Atwood concludes his series on software estimation by arguing that good estimates should be derived from actual counts rather than pure guesswork. Using an anecdote about estimators at a conference, he illustrates how the best estimate comes from finding countable data points rather than making judgmental guesses. He advocates for 'back-of-the-envelope' calculations in the tradition of physicist Enrico Fermi, where estimates are built up from known reference points. The post emphasizes that software projects offer many countable elements—from features and use cases to database tables and lines of code—that can anchor more reliable estimates.
key_points: The best estimates are derived from actual counts, not pure judgment—avoid estimating what you can count directly
When you can't get a direct count, compute your estimate from a related count using back-of-the-envelope calculations
Software projects offer numerous countable data points: requirements, features, function points, database tables, lines of code, test cases, and more
Historical project data is invaluable for anchoring future estimates
The Fermi method of building estimates from known reference points is a powerful but underutilized technique in software development
money_quotes: You should avoid estimating what you can count.
The absolute worst estimates you can produce are judgmental – estimates that are not derived from an actual count of any kind.
The real art of software estimation, then, is the frantic search for data points to hang your estimates on.
Did you guess? Or did you estimate?

His calculations were just as simple as those about the Mississippi River and much more revealing. They showed that, under generous assumptions, the proposed system could work only if there were at least a hundred and twenty seconds in each minute.
Back-of-the-envelope calculations. The idea is standard fare in engineering schools and is bread and butter for most practicing engineers. Unfortunately, it is too often neglected in computing.
key_insight: Good software estimation is not about guessing—it's about finding countable data points and building your estimate from those reference points using systematic calculation.
